perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information includes, without limitation, information, know-how, specifications, materials, models, plans, discoveries, trade secrets (as such term is defined in the Uniform Trade Secrets Act in effect on the Effective Date), records, data, business, marketing, manufacturing and financial records, operations and strategies, invention plans, distribution channels, and technical and product information, customer data, product services, information of the Disclosing Party’s subsidiaries and entities under its control and other communications concerning the Transaction and/or the Disclosing Party’s business and operations, together with all portions of analyses, compilations, notes, studies and other documents prepared by or for the benefit of the Receiving Party which contain or otherwise reflect any of the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information includes, without limitation, information, know-how, specifications, materials, models, plans, discoveries, trade secrets (which shall be defined according to the parties' sole discretion, regardless of any existing state or federal laws), records, data, business, marketing, manufacturing and financial records, operations and strategies, invention plans, distribution channels, and technical and product information, customer data, product services, information of the Disclosing Party’s subsidiaries and entities under its control and other communications concerning the Transaction and/or the Disclosing Party’s business and operations, together with all portions of analyses, compilations, notes, studies and other documents prepared by or for the benefit of the Receiving Party which contain or otherwise reflect any of the foregoing.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the definition of 'trade secrets' is at the parties' sole discretion, the contract attempts to override the statutory definition of trade secrets, which is a legal contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the DUTSA by allowing the parties to define 'trade secrets' outside the scope of the law's established definition, potentially undermining the protection afforded to genuine trade secrets under Delaware law.

Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not b

perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the legal definition of 'trade secret' as defined in the Delaware Code. By stating that the definition of 'trade secrets' is at the parties' sole discretion, the contract attempts to circumvent the statutory definition provided in 6 Del. C. § 2001(4), which specifies that a 'trade secret' must derive independent economic value from not being generally known. The modified text allows for a definition that might not meet this requirement, thereby undermining the protections afforded to legitimate trade secrets under Delaware law. The Perturbation explanation also states that the contract attempts to override the statutory definition of trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that for a period of two (2) years from the date of this Agreement, neither it nor any of its Representatives will, directly or indirectly, solicit for employment or employ any individual serving as an officer of the Disclosing Party or any employee of the Disclosing Party or any of its subsidiaries, in each case with whom the Receiving Party has had substantial contact during its investigation of the Disclosing Party and its business, in each case without obtaining the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party; provided that the Receiving Party may make general solicitations for employment not specifically directed at the Disclosing Party or any of its subsidiaries or their respective employees (including, without limitation, by a bona fide search firm) and solicit and employ (i) any person who responds to such general solicitations, and (ii) any person with whom the Receiving Party was discussing employment prior to the date of this Agreement or with whom the Receiving Party initiates discussions regarding employment after such person is no longer an employee of the Disclosing Party or its subsidiaries.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that for a period of two (2) years from the date of this Agreement, neither it nor any of its Representatives will, directly or indirectly, solicit for employment or employ any individual serving as an officer of the Disclosing Party or any employee of the Disclosing Party or any of its subsidiaries, in each case with whom the Receiving Party has had any contact, however minimal, during its investigation of the Disclosing Party and its business, in each case without obtaining the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party; provided that the Receiving Party may make general solicitations for employment not specifically directed at the Disclosing Party or any of its subsidiaries or their respective employees (including, without limitation, by a bona fide search firm) and solicit and employ (i) any person who responds to such general solicitations, and (ii) any person with whom the Receiving Party was discussing employment prior to the date of this Agreement or with whom the Receiving Party initiates discussions regarding employment after such person is no longer an employee of the Disclosing Party or its subsidiaries.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'substantial contact' to 'any contact, however minimal' broadens the scope of the non-solicitation agreement beyond what is reasonably enforceable and could be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint of trade.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text could violate the Delaware Antitrust Act if the non-solicitation clause, by including 'any contact, however minimal,' is deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade or competition. Delaware law prohibits contracts that unreasonably restrain trade.

perturbation.justification: The modification from 'substantial contact' to 'any contact, however minimal' broadens the scope of the non-solicitation clause. This raises concerns under Delaware's Antitrust Act, which prohibits contracts that unreasonably restrain trade. Scraped Law Snippet 1, § 2103 (a), states: 'Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce of this State shall be unlawful.' The altered text could be interpreted as an unreasonable restraint because it potentially restricts individuals with even minimal contact from seeking employment, thus limiting economic opportunity, which contradicts the purpose and intent detailed in § 2101.

perturbation.original_text: Each party assures the other that it does not intend to, and will not, export or re-export any technical information received under this Agreement, including but not limited to Confidential Information, without compliance with all export control regulations applicable to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party assures the other that it does not intend to, and will not, export or re-export any technical information received under this Agreement, including but not limited to Confidential Information, without obtaining explicit written consent from the Disclosing Party, regardless of compliance with export control regulations.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that export is prohibited without written consent *regardless* of export control regulations contradicts the established framework of export control laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The EAR dictates under what conditions exports are permissible; this change gives absolute export control to the Disclosing Party, irrespective of what the EAR states. The EAR outlines specific licensing requirements and exceptions for exporting certain technologies. This contradicts the EAR by implying that even if an export is permissible under federal regulations, it is still prohibited without the Disclosing Party's consent.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by stating that export is prohibited without written consent from the Disclosing Party, *regardless* of compliance with export control regulations. This directly conflicts with the Export Administration Regulations (EAR), which outlines specific conditions under which exports are permissible. The Law Explanation states that 'The EAR dictates under what conditions exports are permissible; this change gives absolute export control to the Disclosing Party, irrespective of what the EAR states.' Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to the 'Export Administration Regulations' under 15 CFR Chapter VII Subchapter C, indicating that these regulations govern export controls, and the modified contract language attempts to supersede these regulations. The change removes the applicability of established export control laws, giving the Disclosing Party absolute control even if the export would otherwise be allowed.

perturbation.original_text: Each party hereto consents to personal jurisdiction in that State and voluntarily submits to the jurisdiction of the federal and state courts located in the Delaware in any action or proceeding with respect to this Agreement and each party irrevocably waives the right to assert the doctrine of forum non conveniens or a similar doctrine or to object to venue with respect to any action or proceeding brought in any such court.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party hereto consents to personal jurisdiction in that State and voluntarily submits to the jurisdiction of the federal and state courts located in the Delaware in any action or proceeding with respect to this Agreement; however, either party may revoke this consent at their sole discretion at any point during litigation.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a clause allowing parties to revoke their consent to jurisdiction undermines the legal certainty provided by a forum selection clause and contradicts the principle of consent to jurisdiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of contract law regarding forum selection clauses
perturbation.law_citation: M/S Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co., 407 U.S. 1 (1972)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Supreme Court case M/S Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co. established the enforceability of forum selection clauses. Allowing a party to unilaterally revoke consent to jurisdiction directly contradicts the established legal precedent of enforcing forum selection clauses, as it creates uncertainty and undermines the predictability of contractual agreements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: The Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co. | 407 U.S. 1 (1972) | Justia U.S. Supreme Court Center The Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co., 407 U.S. 1 (1972) Overview Opinions Materials Argued: March 21, 1972 Decided: June 12, 1972 Syllabus U.S. Supreme Court The Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co., 407 U.S. 1 (1972) The Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co. No. 71-322 Argued March 21, 1972 Decided June 12, 1972 407 U.S. 1 Syllabus Petitioner Unterweser made an agreement to tow respondent's drilling rig from Louisiana to Italy. The contract contained a forum-selection clause providing for the litigation of any dispute in the High Court of Justice in London. When the rig under tow was damaged in a storm, respondent instructed Unterweser to tow the rig to Tampa, the nearest port of refuge. There, respondent brought suit in admiralty against petitioners. Unterweser invoked the forum clause in moving for dismissal for want of jurisdiction and brought suit in the English court, which ruled that it had jurisdiction under the contractual forum provision. The District Court, relying on Carbon Black Export, Inc. v. The Monrosa, 254 F.2d 297, held the forum-selection clause unenforceable, and refused to decline jurisdiction on the basis of forum non conveniens. The Court of Appeals affirmed. Held: The forum-selection clause, which was a vital part of the towing contract, is binding on the parties unless respondent can meet the heavy burden of showing that its enforcement would be unreasonable, unfair, or unjust. Pp. 407 U. S. 8 -20 428 F.2d 888 and 446 F.2d 907, vacated and remanded. BURGER, C. J., delivered the opinion of the Court, in which BRENNAN, STEWART, WHITE, MARSHALL, BLACKMUN, POWELL, and REHNQUIST, JJ., joined. WHITE, J., filed a concurring statement, post, p. 407 U. S. 20 . DOUGLAS, J., filed a dissenting opinion, post, p. 407 U. S. 20 . Page 407 U. S. 2 Read More Opinions Opinions & Dissents U.S. Supreme Court The Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co., 407 U.S. 1 (1972) The Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co. No. 71-322 Argued March 21, 1972 Decided Ju
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by allowing either party to revoke consent to jurisdiction at any point during litigation. This directly undermines the enforceability of the forum selection clause, which the Supreme Court case M/S Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co. established. The original text ensures both parties consent to the jurisdiction, while the changed text allows unilateral revocation, destabilizing the agreement and contradicting the legal precedent supporting forum selection clauses. The scraped snippet from The Bremen v. Zapata Off-Shore Co. case highlights the importance of forum-selection clauses in contracts, stating that such a clause 'is binding on the parties unless respondent can meet the heavy burden of showing that its enforcement would be unreasonable, unfair, or unjust.' The modified text circumvents this binding agreement by allowing revocation, creating a significant contradiction.
